items.notes: Pronounced 'RA-ha'. One of the most essential words for daily life. Partitive: rahaa. Plural: rahat (also means 'funds').
examples: Minulla on rahaa. — I have money.
Rahat ovat lompakossa. — The money is in the wallet.

items.notes: Pronounced 'EU-ro'. Finland adopted the euro in 2002. Partitive: euroa. 'Viisi euroa' = five euros.
examples: Se maksaa viisi euroa. — It costs five euros.
Onko sinulla euroa? — Do you have a euro?

items.notes: Pronounced 'SENT-ti'. Note the double 't'. Partitive: senttiä. Used with euro: 'kaksi euroa ja viisikymmentä senttiä'.
examples: Hinta on viisikymmentä senttiä. — The price is fifty cents.
Minulla ei ole yhtään senttiä. — I don't have a single cent.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HIN-ta'. Partitive: hintaa. 'Mikä on hinta?' = What is the price? Stem changes in some cases: hinnan (genitive).
examples: Mikä on tämän hinta? — What is the price of this?
Hinta on liian korkea. — The price is too high.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAL-pa'. Comparative: halvempi (cheaper). Superlative: halvin (cheapest). Note the stem change: halpa → halvan (genitive).
examples: Tämä on halpa. — This is cheap.
Haluaisin halvemman vaihtoehdon. — I would like a cheaper option.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAL-lis'. Comparative: kalliimpi. Superlative: kallein. Stem: kallii- in some forms. Opposite of 'halpa'.
examples: Tämä on liian kallis. — This is too expensive.
Helsinki on kallis kaupunki. — Helsinki is an expensive city.

items.notes: Pronounced 'IL-mai-nen'. Follows -inen adjective pattern. Partitive: ilmaista. Means free of charge, not freedom (vapaa). 'Ilmaiseksi' = for free (translative).
examples: Tämä on ilmainen. — This is free.
Sain sen ilmaiseksi. — I got it for free.

items.notes: Pronounced 'A-len-nus'. Derived from 'alentaa' (to reduce). Stem: alennukse-. 'Alennusmyynti' = clearance sale. Partitive: alennusta.
examples: Onko tässä alennusta? — Is there a discount on this?
Sain kymmenen prosentin alennuksen. — I got a ten percent discount.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TAR-jo-us'. Stem: tarjoukse-. Used both for shop deals and formal offers/proposals. 'Tarjouksessa' = on offer/on sale.
examples: Tämä tuote on tarjouksessa. — This product is on offer.
Hyvä tarjous! — Good deal!

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAS-si'. Note the double 's'. A shopping bag or tote bag. Partitive: kassia. 'Muovikassi' = plastic bag, 'kangaskassi' = cloth bag.
examples: Tarvitsetko kassin? — Do you need a bag?
Kassi on täynnä. — The bag is full.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KUEET-ti'. Note the double 'i' and double 't'. Partitive: kuittia. You may be asked 'Haluatko kuitin?' (Do you want the receipt?) at checkout.
examples: Haluatko kuitin? — Do you want the receipt?
Tarvitsen kuitin palautusta varten. — I need the receipt for a return.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAS-sa'. Note the double 's'. Can mean both the machine and the checkout area. 'Itsepalvelukassa' = self-checkout. Not to be confused with 'kassi' (bag).
examples: Kassa on tuolla. — The checkout is over there.
Mene kassalle. — Go to the checkout.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MYY-jä'. Derived from 'myydä' (to sell). The -jä suffix indicates the person performing the action. Gender-neutral. Partitive: myyjää.
examples: Myyjä auttoi minua. — The salesperson helped me.
Kysy myyjältä. — Ask the salesperson.

items.notes: Pronounced 'A-si-a-kas'. Important stem change: asiakas → asiakkaa- (e.g., asiakkaan = customer's, asiakkaita = customers). Partitive: asiakasta.
examples: Asiakas on aina oikeassa. — The customer is always right.
Meillä on paljon asiakkaita. — We have many customers.

items.notes: Pronounced 'OS-tos'. Derived from 'ostaa' (to buy). Stem: ostokse-. Plural 'ostokset' commonly means 'shopping/groceries'. 'Tehdä ostoksia' = to do shopping.
examples: Tein hyvän ostoksen. — I made a good purchase.
Ostokset ovat kassissa. — The shopping is in the bag.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MAK-su'. Derived from 'maksaa' (to pay). Partitive: maksua. 'Korttimaksu' = card payment, 'käteismaksu' = cash payment. 'Maksuton' = free of charge.
examples: Maksu onnistui. — The payment was successful.
Hyväksyttekö korttimaksun? — Do you accept card payment?

items.notes: Pronounced 'KO-lik-ko'. Note the double 'k'. Partitive: kolikkoa. Plural: kolikot. Finnish euro coins feature various national designs.
examples: Tarvitsen kolikon ostoskärryyn. — I need a coin for the shopping cart.
Minulla on vain kolikoita. — I only have coins.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SE-te-li'. Partitive: seteliä. Plural: setelit. Euro banknotes are shared across the eurozone. 'Viiden euron seteli' = five euro note.
examples: Onko sinulla pienempää seteliä? — Do you have a smaller banknote?
Sain vaihtorahaksi setelin. — I got a banknote as change.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LOM-pak-ko'. Note the double 'k'. Partitive: lompakkoa. Stem: lompakko- → lompakon (genitive). A common everyday item.
examples: Lompakkoni on taskussa. — My wallet is in my pocket.
Unohdin lompakon kotiin. — I forgot my wallet at home.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TO-ri'. Partitive: toria. Finnish cities have famous market squares: 'Kauppatori' in Helsinki is the most well-known.
examples: Mennään torille! — Let's go to the market!
Ostin mansikoita torilta. — I bought strawberries from the market.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAUP-pa-kes-kus'. Compound word: kauppa (shop) + keskus (center). Partitive: kauppakeskusta. Major ones in Helsinki include Kamppi and Itäkeskus.
examples: Kauppakeskus avautuu kello yhdeksältä. — The shopping center opens at nine o'clock.
Tapaamme kauppakeskuksessa. — Let's meet at the shopping center.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VAA-te'. Partitive: vaatetta. Plural 'vaatteet' means 'clothes' in general. 'Vaatekauppa' = clothing store.
examples: Tarvitsen uusia vaatteita. — I need new clothes.
Vaatekauppa on toisessa kerroksessa. — The clothing store is on the second floor.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KO-ko'. Partitive: kokoa. Used for clothing sizes, shoe sizes, and general size. 'Mikä koko?' = What size? Also means 'whole/entire' as an adjective.
examples: Mikä koko tämä on? — What size is this?
Onko tätä isompaa kokoa? — Is there a bigger size of this?

items.notes: Pronounced 'LAH-ja'. Partitive: lahjaa. 'Lahjakortti' = gift card. 'Joululahja' = Christmas present. 'Syntymäpäivälahja' = birthday present.
examples: Tämä on lahja sinulle. — This is a gift for you.
Ostin joululahjoja. — I bought Christmas presents.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PAK-ka-us'. Stem: pakkaukse-. Derived from 'pakata' (to pack). Can mean packaging, parcel, or pack. 'Pakkausmateriaali' = packaging material.
examples: Pakkaus on avattu. — The package has been opened.
Haluatko lahjapakkauksen? — Do you want gift wrapping?

items.notes: Pronounced 'A-le'. Short colloquial form of 'alennus' (discount). Very commonly used in Finnish shops and advertising. 'Alessa' = on sale.
examples: Kaupassa on ale! — There is a sale at the store!
Ostin takin alesta. — I bought a jacket on sale.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KÄ-tei-nen'. Literally means 'in hand' (from 'käsi' = hand). Used as a noun meaning cash money. 'Käteisellä' = with cash (adessive). Follows -inen adjective pattern.
examples: Maksatko käteisellä vai kortilla? — Do you pay with cash or card?
Minulla ei ole käteistä. — I don't have cash.
